claimed_contribution: Provides an initial empirical examination of how multiple social identities, especially chronic self-construal and cause-related identity, interact to shape consumer preference between donation-based and discount-based promotions.
Shows that key moderators and boundary conditions of these effects include identity congruence, identity salience, charity efficiency, and product type.
Offers preliminary evidence that promotion choice can feed back to identity by changing postchoice moral identity accessibility, particularly for independent consumers.
context: The paper studies cause-related marketing in which firms can offer either cents-off discounts to buyers or equivalent charitable donations tied to purchase. The empirical contexts are consumer choices for everyday products (e.g., bottled water, cereal, beverages, coffee/tea, snack bars) with local or nonlocal charity tie-ins.
design_and_data: The authors report five experiments. Study 1 (N = 168 undergraduates) uses a between-subjects design comparing discount, identity-congruent donation, and identity-incongruent donation promotions, with measured chronic self-construal. Studies 2a (N = 225) and 2b (N = 252) test identity salience and cause congruence while comparing donation versus discount preferences; Study 2b also manipulates self-construal and uses multiple products and incentive amounts. Study 3 (N = 133 university staff and students) uses an actual coupon choice task and manipulates charity efficiency (2-star vs 4-star rating), and Study 4 (N = 168 undergraduates) tests indulgent versus healthy product contexts and measures postchoice identity accessibility.
main_findings: When the donation cause was identity-congruent and self-construal was relatively salient, interdependent consumers preferred donation promotions more, whereas independent consumers showed stronger preference for discounts.
The self-construal effect was attenuated when the donation cause was identity-incongruent and when a cause-related identity was more salient than self-construal during choice.

Interdependent consumers' tendency to choose donations disappeared when the charity was framed as inefficient, and independent consumers' discount preference weakened for indulgent products; after choosing a donation, independent consumers reported higher moral identity accessibility than interdependent consumers.
one_line_summary: Across five experiments, the paper shows that donation-versus-discount promotion preferences are shaped by interactions between self-construal and cause-related identity, with clear boundary conditions from identity salience/congruence, charity efficiency, and product type.
relation_to_prior_work: extends prior promotions and identity research by integrating self-construal and specific cause-related identity in the same framework for donation-versus-discount choice. It refines earlier donations-versus-discounts findings by identifying when contextual moderators change these preference patterns.
research_gap: Prior work had examined charitable appeal responses and traditional deal evaluations separately, but had not tested how multiple identities jointly influence preference between donation-based and discount-based promotions. The authors also note that evidence on when and why donations outperform discounts is still limited and needs clearer boundary conditions.
research_question: How do consumers' chronic self-construals and cause-related social identities jointly affect preferences for donation-based versus discount-based promotions? Under what conditions do identity congruence, identity salience, charity efficiency, and product type strengthen or attenuate these effects?
